narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 37, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3, Year 3 decile of 2, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time—solid foundations in many ways, especially your LSAT which is right around the typical student's score. The admissions model, designed to predict first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 68.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which fell well below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lower third versus the typical mid-range, reinforced this concern as the next biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out positively, pulling the odds in favor of passing since it matched or slightly exceeded what successful students usually bring. Your undergraduate GPA, a bit lower than the average 3.21, added a small nudge toward the failure prediction, while your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered minor boosts by aligning with traits of bar passers. Overall, your academic deciles in school years were the standout hurdles, placing you below the stronger profiles of students who typically pass the bar on their first try. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted growth. Next, consider retaking courses or prep programs to boost your decile rankings, honing LSAT-level skills further, and seeking mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated this path. You're resilient, and with focused steps on those key academics, you can reshape this story and reapply stronger. We're rooting for you.
